title: Al-Mamnuʿ min al-Sarf (the Diptote — Restricted from Full Declension)

sections.text: Al-Mamnuʿ min al-Sarf (the Diptote)

Al-mamnuʿ min al-sarf (the diptote) is an inflected (muʿrab) noun that does not accept tanwin and is put in the genitive with fatha (instead of kasra) — except when it is annexed (mudaf) or carries the definite article 'al-', in which case it is put in the genitive with kasra.
Al-sarf (full declension): the tanwin attached to the end of an inflected noun. A 'masruf' noun is one that accepts tanwin; a 'mamnuʿ min al-sarf' noun is one that does not accept it.

items.text: — I greeted Ahmad. (genitive with fatha — substituting for kasra)

sections.text: In the third example, 'Ahmad' is in the genitive with fatha (substituting for kasra) because it is mamnuʿ min al-sarf. It does not carry tanwin in any of the examples because it does not accept tanwin.
Categories of Al-Mamnuʿ min al-Sarf

Al-mamnuʿ min al-sarf is divided into three main categories: those restricted for two reasons (ʿillatayn); those restricted for one reason; and the descriptive adjective restricted from full declension.
First: Restricted for Two Reasons

A noun is restricted for two reasons when it is a proper name (ʿalam) bearing — together with proper-name-ness — another reason; or a descriptive adjective bearing — together with descriptive-ness — another reason.
1 — The Proper Name (ʿAlam)

A proper name is restricted from full declension when it bears any of the following reasons: feminine-ness, foreignness (ʿujma), addition of alif and nun, the pattern of a verb, deviation from original form (ʿadl), or compound-blend composition.
(a) The Feminine Proper Name
A proper name is restricted from full declension if it is feminine — whether feminine in form (containing the taʾ of the feminine), in meaning (referring to a female even without taʾ), or both.

items.text: — Muʿawiya visited al-Sham. (formally feminine, of a male)

sections.text: A three-letter feminine proper name with sukun on the middle letter — if it is foreign or transferred from a masculine name — is obligatorily mamnuʿ min al-sarf. If it is Arabic and not transferred, both treatments (full declension and restriction) are permissible, with full declension being preferable. E.g., 'Hind' permits both.
(b) The Foreign Proper Name
A foreign proper name is restricted from full declension if it has more than three letters. If it is three-letter and has sukun on the middle letter, it is masruf (fully declined).

items.text: — Ibrahim emigrated to al-Sham. (foreign, more than three letters)
— Allah spoke to Moses. (foreign, more letters)
— Noah came. (foreign three-letter, sukun on middle — masruf)

sections.text: (c) The Proper Name Ending in Added Alif and Nun
A proper name is restricted from full declension if it ends in an added alif and nun, e.g., ʿUthman, Sulayman, Ramadan.

items.text: — Allah sent Sulayman as a prophet.

sections.text: (d) The Proper Name on the Pattern of a Verb
A proper name is restricted from full declension if it is on a pattern specific to verbs or one predominantly used for verbs, e.g., Ahmad, Yazid, Shammar, Taghlib.

items.text: — [The tribe of] Taghlib settled in al-Jazira.

sections.text: (e) The Deviated Proper Name (Al-ʿAlam al-Maʿdul)
Al-ʿalam al-maʿdul: one that has been deviated from its original form to another — e.g., 'ʿAmir' becomes 'ʿUmar', 'Zafir' becomes 'Zufar'.
(f) The Proper Name with Compound-Blend Composition
A proper name is restricted from full declension if it is composed by mazj (compound-blend), not ending in 'wayh', e.g., Baʿlabakk, Hadramawt, New York.
2 — The Descriptive Adjective
An adjective is restricted for two reasons when, together with descriptive-ness, it bears another reason: addition of alif and nun, the pattern of a verb, or deviation from original form.
(a) Adjective Ending in Added Alif and Nun
An adjective is restricted from full declension if it is on the pattern faʿlan whose feminine is faʿla, e.g., ʿatshan ('thirsty'; fem. ʿatsha), juʿan (fem. juʿa), ghadban (fem. ghadba).
(b) Adjective on the Pattern afʿal

An adjective is restricted from full declension if it is on the pattern afʿal whose feminine is faʿlaʾ, e.g., ahmar ('red'; fem. hamraʾ), abyad (fem. baydaʾ), aʿraj ('lame'; fem. ʿarjaʾ), akbar (fem. kubra).

items.text: — I asked about a more distant place.

sections.text: (c) The Deviated Adjective

An adjective is restricted from full declension if it is deviated from its original form, in two patterns: 'mafʿal' deviated from 'faʿil' (e.g., mathna, thulatha, rubaʿa — 'two by two', 'three by three', 'four by four'); and 'fuʿal' deviated from 'faʿil' (e.g., ukhar — 'others').

items.text: — The students came two by two, three by three, and four by four.

sections.text: Second: Restricted for One Reason

A noun is restricted from full declension for one reason if it ends in alif al-taʾnith al-maqsura (the shortened feminine alif), or alif al-taʾnith al-mamduda (the extended feminine alif), or is on the 'extreme plural' pattern (sighat muntaha al-jumuʿ).
1 — Ending in Alif al-Taʾnith al-Maqsura
Alif al-taʾnith al-maqsura: an added alif at the end of a noun indicating femininity, e.g., dhikra ('memory'), kubra ('greater [fem.]'), Salma, hubla ('pregnant'), ʿatsha ('thirsty').

A noun ending in alif al-taʾnith al-maqsura shows no overt iʿrab change — it is mabni in appearance in all states, with implied vowel-marks on the alif. Its restriction from full declension shows only in the absence of tanwin.
2 — Ending in Alif al-Taʾnith al-Mamduda

Alif al-taʾnith al-mamduda: an added alif followed by hamza at the end of a noun indicating femininity, e.g., sahraʾ ('desert'), hasnaʾ ('beautiful [fem.]'), hamraʾ ('red [fem.]'), asdiqaʾ ('friends').

items.text: — I walked in a vast desert.

sections.text: 3 — Nouns on the Extreme Plural Pattern

Sighat muntaha al-jumuʿ (the 'extreme plural' pattern): every broken plural after whose alif there are two or three letters with a sukun in the middle. The most well-known patterns: mafaʿil, mafaʿil, faʿalil, faʿalil.

items.text: — Many mosques have been built in the city.
— Small sparrows fly in the sky.

sections.text: Iʿrab of Al-Mamnuʿ min al-Sarf
Al-mamnuʿ min al-sarf takes nominative with damma; accusative with fatha; and genitive with fatha (substituting for kasra). It does not accept tanwin in any of its states.
When Is Al-Mamnuʿ min al-Sarf Put in the Genitive with Kasra?
Al-mamnuʿ min al-sarf is put in the genitive with kasra (returning to its original) in only two cases: if it is annexed (mudaf), or if it carries the definite article 'al-'.

items.text: — I prayed in the mosques. ('al-' attached)
— I prayed in the mosques of the city. (mudaf)
— I prayed in many mosques. (no 'al-' and not mudaf — genitive with fatha)

sections.text: Why does al-mamnuʿ min al-sarf return to genitive with kasra when annexed or carrying 'al-'?

The reason is that restriction from full declension is a secondary status of the noun, while genitive with kasra is the original iʿrab. When the noun is annexed or carries 'al-', the cause restricting it is lightened, so it returns to its original and is put in the genitive with kasra. As for tanwin, it does not return even with annexation or 'al-', because both of those originally prevent tanwin in all nouns.
Causes of Restriction from Full Declension — Summary

headers: Restricted for One or Two Reasons

sections.text: Example of Iʿrab

items.text: — The Muslims prayed in many mosques.

sections.text: salla: a past-tense verb fixed on an estimated fatha. al-muslimuna: the subject in the nominative, marked by waw because it is a sound masculine plural. fi: a preposition. masajid-a: a noun in the genitive after 'fi', marked by fatha (substituting for kasra) because it is mamnuʿ min al-sarf on the extreme plural pattern. kathira: a naʿt in the genitive, marked by the manifest kasra.
The student should distinguish: a mamnuʿ min al-sarf noun in the genitive is put in the genitive with fatha if it is neither annexed nor carrying 'al-'; with kasra if it is annexed or carrying 'al-'.
Allah Most High said: "And We have certainly beautified the nearest heaven with stars (masabih)..." — Quran 67:5 (Sahih International)

In this noble verse, 'masabiha' is in the genitive after the 'baʾ', marked by fatha (substituting for kasra) because it is mamnuʿ min al-sarf on the extreme plural pattern. It does not carry 'al-' and is not annexed, so it remains restricted from full declension.
And Allah Most High said: "Indeed, the righteous will be in pleasure. On thrones (al-araʾiki), observing." — Quran 83:22-23 (Sahih International)

In His saying 'al-araʾik-i', the noun is put in the genitive with kasra — not with fatha — even though it is on the extreme plural pattern. This is because 'al-' is attached to it, returning it to the original genitive with kasra.
